    Magnetic Fields in Astrophysics

    Magnetic Fields

    Type:Foundational concept
    Typical ISM strengths:1–10 μG (diffuse) to mG (dense cores)
    Key diagnostics:Dust polarimetry, Faraday rotation, Zeeman effect
    Related: B-fields in star formation
    Interstellar medium
    Turbulence

    Magnetic fields pervade the universe on all scales, from planetary magnetospheres to the vast intergalactic medium. In astrophysics, magnetic fields are generated and amplified by dynamo processes in conducting plasmas, and once established they influence gas dynamics, regulate star formation, channel outflows and jets, and shape the structure of the interstellar medium.

    Origin and Amplification

    Astrophysical magnetic fields are thought to originate from weak seed fields in the early universe, subsequently amplified by dynamo processes — the conversion of kinetic energy from plasma motions into magnetic energy. In galaxies, the α–Ω dynamo operates through the combination of differential rotation (Ω effect) and helical turbulence (α effect) to maintain large-scale ordered fields of a few μG.

    Magnetic Fields in the ISM

    In the diffuse ISM, B-field strengths are typically 1–10 μG. As gas contracts into molecular clouds, flux freezing (the coupling of field lines to the ionized gas component) causes the field to strengthen roughly as B ∝ n^(0.5–0.65) at densities above ~300 cm⁻³[1]. In the densest protostellar cores, fields reach milligauss levels.

    The dynamical importance of the magnetic field is quantified by the mass-to-flux ratio — the ratio of gravitational mass to magnetic flux, normalized to the critical value above which gravity overwhelms magnetic support[1].

    Observational Techniques

    • Dust polarimetry — aligned dust grains emit polarized thermal radiation; rotating the polarization angle by 90° reveals the plane-of-sky B-field direction
    • Zeeman effect — splitting of spectral lines by the magnetic field gives the line-of-sight field strength directly; the most direct measurement but requires strong fields or very sensitive observations[1]
    • Faraday rotation — rotation of the polarization angle of background radio sources through a magnetized plasma; traces B-fields in ionized gas
    • Synchrotron polarimetry — polarized radio emission from relativistic electrons spiraling around field lines; traces fields in supernova remnants and AGN jets
    • Davis-Chandrasekhar-Fermi method — estimates B-field strength from polarization angle dispersion combined with gas density and velocity dispersion

    Astrophysical Contexts

    • Star formation — B-fields regulate collapse, generate hourglass morphologies, and launch protostellar outflows
    • Galaxy evolution — large-scale galactic fields influence gas flows, cosmic ray propagation, and ram-pressure stripping
    • Galaxy clusters — μG-level fields in the intracluster medium affect thermal conduction and particle acceleration at shocks
    • AGN jets — highly ordered fields collimate relativistic jets over kpc scales

    See Also

    • Magnetic fields in star formation — detailed role in cloud collapse
    • Dust polarimetry — primary B-field mapping technique
    • DCF method — B-field strength estimation
    • Mass-to-flux ratio — magnetic criticality diagnostic
    • Interstellar medium

    References
    1. Crutcher, R. M. (2012). "Magnetic Fields in Molecular Clouds." ARA&A, 50, 29. DOI:10.1146/annurev-astro-081811-125514
